attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll run the Refactor phase. Let me start by reading the architecture notes, implementation, and tests.

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted module-level constant `PROCESSING_FEE = 5` in `src/claim-office.ts`; `quote()` now returns `PROCESSING_FEE`
5	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `5` is the domain "processing fee" from the spec; naming it removes a magic number
6	- Naming established: `PROCESSING_FEE` (module-private constant); `quote(input)` public function kept as-is — name still fits
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- No refactoring (justified): only 2 data points (empty=5, single potion newcomer=49); the literal `49` is hardcoded and any decomposition into `base + first-insurance + fee` components would be speculative triangulation beyond what tests demand. Naming `49` (e.g. `POTION_NEWCOMER_PREMIUM`) would not add intent beyond the test name and would clutter the module before the algorithm emerges in cycle 3+ (amulet test). `quote` name still fits.
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: replaced `if (type === "amulet") return 71; return 49;` chain with a `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE: Record<string, number>` lookup table (`potion: 49, amulet: 71`); `quote` now does a single map lookup on `items[0].type`
13	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — premium varies per item type, so a per-type lookup is the natural shape and removes a conditional chain that would have grown with staff/sword. Also nets Rule 4 (one fewer conditional, mass 23→22). Kept totals (49, 71) un-decomposed: tests do not yet pin down the `base + base/10 + fee` formula; decomposition deferred to cycle 4 (staff) or 5 (sword) when a 3rd/4th data point makes the pattern undeniable
14	- Naming established: `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (module-private lookup); `quote` name still fits
15

content.text:
Tests are green. Now let me evaluate the refactor.

**Naming Evaluation**:
- `quote(input)`: clear, matches domain term ("quote/premium calculation") — keep
- `PROCESSING_FEE`: clear — keep
- `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE`: clear, accurately describes the lookup — keep

**Current Code Mass**:
```typescript
const PROCESSING_FEE = 5;                              // const 1, binding 1 = 2
const NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE: Record<string, number> = {
  potion: 49, amulet: 71, staff: 93,                   // 3 keys (binding) + 3 vals (const) = 6
};                                                     // binding 1 = 1
function quote(input: ...): number {                   // binding 1 (input) = 1
  if (input.items.length === 0) return PROCESSING_FEE; // cond 4 + invoc.length 2 + binding input.items 1 + const 0 1 + binding PROCESSING_FEE 1 = 9
  return NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE[input.items[0].type]; // invocation [] 2 + binding NEWCOMER... 1 + binding input.items 1 + const 0 1 + binding type 1 = 6
}
```
Approximate total mass ≈ 25.

**Evaluate Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)** — Looking at the test names with formula breakdowns visible:
- potion: 40 base + 4 first-insurance + 5 fee = 49
- amulet: 60 base + 6 first-insurance + 5 fee = 71
- staff: 80 base + 8 first-insurance + 5 fee = 93

With 3 data points now firmly aligned with the formula `base + base/10 + fee`, we can decompose to reveal the actual domain intent rather than hiding the formula behind opaque totals. The architecture notes from cycle 3 even called this out: "decomposition deferred to cycle 4 (staff) or 5 (sword) when a 3rd/4th data point makes the pattern undeniable." We now have the 3rd data point.

However, the upcoming sword test (cycle 5) at 115 = 100+10+5 would also fit, AND the upcoming rune test introduces 33 = 25 + 2.5 + 5 with rounding — which would force a `Math.ceil` for the first-insurance component. Decomposing now into `base + Math.round/Math.ceil(base * 0.1) + PROCESSING_FEE` would be speculative regarding the rounding rule, and would replace a tested map-lookup (mass 6) with arithmetic + lookup (higher mass).

But we can take a **conservative** decomposition step that's clearly justified by current evidence: extract the per-type **base** prices into a `BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` map, and compute the newcomer premium as `base + base/10 + PROCESSING_FEE`. With three matching data points (40→49, 60→71, 80→93, and 80/10=8 is integer), this passes all four tests without speculation. Wait — let me verify: 40+4+5=49 ✓, 60+6+5=71 ✓, 80+8+5=93 ✓. Yes, all integer arithmetic works.

Tradeoffs:
- **Pro (Rule 2)**: The code would now express the actual formula `base + base/10 + fee` from the domain spec, not just opaque totals
- **Pro (Rule 3)**: Removes conceptual duplication — the +5 fee and 10% surcharge are implicit in every premium total today
- **Con (mass)**: Adds an invocation and an addition (higher mass)
- **Risk**: The rounding for rune (2.5→3? or 2.5→2?) is unknown; but this refactor only handles the integer cases tested so far, and the spec test names confirm the formula

This is a meaningful Rule 2 improvement — making the **first-insurance surcharge** and **base price** explicit domain concepts rather than precomputed totals. Let me apply it.

content.text:
All 4 tests still pass.

**Refactored Code Mass** (approximate):
- 2 simple constants (PROCESSING_FEE, FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE): ~4
- BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE map (3 keys + 3 vals + binding): ~7
- quote function: binding input, conditional, return PROCESSING_FEE, 2 assignments (const basePrice/firstInsuranceSurcharge are bindings not mutations → mass 1 each), invocations (.length, []), multiplication (invoc), addition (invoc x2), bindings → ~22

Total ≈ 33 (up from ~25). Mass increased, but Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP.

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Decomposed opaque per-type totals (49/71/93) into the domain formula `base + base × 10% first-insurance surcharge + processing fee`. The test names explicitly describe this decomposition (e.g. "49 G (40 base + 4 first-insurance + 5 fee)"), so the implementation now mirrors the spec language.
- Introduced `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.1` as a named domain constant
- Renamed `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` → `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` to reflect that it now holds the base price, not the precomputed premium
- Rule 3 (DRY): Removed conceptual duplication — the `+ surcharge + fee` pattern was implicit in every map entry; now expressed once

**Tradeoffs accepted**:
- Mass increased (~25 → ~33). Rule 2 trumps APP per the priority order.
- Why this is safe with only 3 data points: all three (40→49, 60→71, 80→93) produce exact integers under `base + base*0.1 + 5`, and the upcoming sword test (100→115) also fits. Rounding rules for the rune case (25→33) are not yet asserted, so this decomposition only commits to what the current 4 passing tests demand.

Now let me update the architecture notes.

## After cycle 3
- Change: replaced `if (type === "amulet") return 71; return 49;` chain with a `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE: Record<string, number>` lookup table (`potion: 49, amulet: 71`); `quote` now does a single map lookup on `items[0].type`
- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — premium varies per item type, so a per-type lookup is the natural shape and removes a conditional chain that would have grown with staff/sword. Also nets Rule 4 (one fewer conditional, mass 23→22). Kept totals (49, 71) un-decomposed: tests do not yet pin down the `base + base/10 + fee` formula; decomposition deferred to cycle 4 (staff) or 5 (sword) when a 3rd/4th data point makes the pattern undeniable
- Naming established: `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (module-private lookup); `quote` name still fits
input.new_string:
## After cycle 3
- Change: replaced `if (type === "amulet") return 71; return 49;` chain with a `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE: Record<string, number>` lookup table (`potion: 49, amulet: 71`); `quote` now does a single map lookup on `items[0].type`
- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — premium varies per item type, so a per-type lookup is the natural shape and removes a conditional chain that would have grown with staff/sword. Also nets Rule 4 (one fewer conditional, mass 23→22). Kept totals (49, 71) un-decomposed: tests do not yet pin down the `base + base/10 + fee` formula; decomposition deferred to cycle 4 (staff) or 5 (sword) when a 3rd/4th data point makes the pattern undeniable
- Naming established: `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (module-private lookup); `quote` name still fits

## After cycle 4
- Change: decomposed opaque per-type totals into the domain formula. `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (49/71/93) → `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (40/60/80) plus new constant `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.1`. `quote` now computes `basePrice + basePrice * 0.1 + PROCESSING_FEE` for non-empty item lists.
- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — with the 3rd data point (staff 80→93) in place, all three test names explicitly state the same `base + first-insurance + fee` decomposition; the implementation now mirrors the spec language instead of hiding the formula behind precomputed totals. Also Rule 3 (DRY): the surcharge+fee pattern was conceptually duplicated across all three map entries; now expressed once. Mass increased ~25→~33 — accepted because Rule 2 > APP. Verified all 3 base prices (40, 60, 80) and the upcoming sword case (100) yield integer results under `base*0.1`, so no rounding decision was committed (rounding rule for rune 25→33 deferred to its own cycle).
- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (replaces `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE`), `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` (new domain constant); `quote` name still fits

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete.

**Refactoring**: Decomposed the per-type premium totals (49/71/93) into the explicit domain formula `basePrice + basePrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE + PROCESSING_FEE`. Renamed `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` → `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (values now 40/60/80) and introduced `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.1`. The implementation now mirrors the spec language visible in the test names ("40 base + 4 first-insurance + 5 fee").

**Mass Change**: ~25 → ~33 (increased — accepted because Rule 2 "reveals intent" trumps APP)

**Tests**: All 4 passing.

**Why this was the right moment**: Cycle 3's architecture note explicitly deferred decomposition until "a 3rd/4th data point makes the pattern undeniable." Staff (cycle 4) is that 3rd data point. All three base prices (40, 60, 80) and the upcoming sword (100) yield integer first-insurance amounts under `base × 0.1`, so the refactor commits to no rounding rule beyond what the current tests assert.
